We now have a dataset that provides the employment state and a confirmation on post-secondary attendance for each PersonID and grad.year.x.
Let’s analyze.



Proportion of individuals per path by grad.year.x
Next we want to figure out the different paths that these group of individuals take. The paths that we are most interested in are the following;

After 2023
Has meaningful employment in same county as high school
Has meaningful employment in same EDR as high school, but not same county
Has meaningful employment in Northwest Minnesota, but not in same EDR as high school.
Has meaningful employment in Minnesota, but not in Northwest Minnesota.
Does not have meaningful employment or a MN employment record, but is attending post-secondary
Does not have meaningful employment and is not attending post-secondary.
Does not have a MN employment record and is not attending post-secondary.

Let’s first build a new dataset where each PersonID has one of these seven states for each grad.year.x.


seven.states <- ps.emp.states %>%
  mutate(states = ifelse(county.match == "Meaningful emp - match", 1, 99),
         states = ifelse(edr.match == "Meaningful emp - match" & county.match != "Meaningful emp - match", 2, states),
         states = ifelse(region.match == "Meaningful emp - match" & county.match != "Meaningful emp - match" & edr.match != "Meaningful emp - match", 3, states),
         states = ifelse(state.match == "Meaningful emp - match" & county.match != "Meaningful emp - match" & edr.match != "Meaningful emp - match" & region.match != "Meaningful emp - match", 4, states),
         states = ifelse(state.match == "After 2023", 0, states),
         states = ifelse(ps.attend == "ps.attend" & !(states %in% c(0,1,2,3,4)), 5, states),
         states = ifelse(ps.attend == "ps.not.attend" & state.match == "MN emp record - not meaningful", 6, states),
         states = ifelse(ps.attend == "ps.not.attend" & state.match == "No MN emp record", 7, states),
         states = ifelse(states == "0", "After 2023", states),
         states = ifelse(states == "1", "Meaningful emp County", states),
         states = ifelse(states == "2", "Meaningful emp EDR", states),
         states = ifelse(states == "3", "Meaningful emp region", states),
         states = ifelse(states == "4", "Meaningful emp MN", states),
         states = ifelse(states == "5", "Attending ps", states),
         states = ifelse(states == "6", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", states),
         states = ifelse(states == "7", "No MN emp record, not attending ps", states),
         states = as.factor(states),
         states = fct_relevel(states, "Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp EDR", "Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps", "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "No MN emp record, not attending ps", "After 2023"))

The new dataset has 1,050,400 rows and 10 with 65,650 unique individuals. The seven states are labeled as the following;

Meaningful emp County: means they have worked 1,000 hours or more for one employer located in the same county as their high school.
Meaningful emp EDR: means that have worked 1,000 hours or more for one employer located in the same EDR as their high school, but not in the same county.
Meaningful emp SW: means they have worked 1,000 hours or more for one employer located in Northwest Minnesota, but not in the same EDR or county as their high school.
Meaningful emp MN - means they have worked 1,000 hours or more for one employer located in Minnesota, but not in Northwest Minnesota.
Attending ps: Means the individuals either has a MN employment record but it isn’t meaningful OR they do not have a MN employment record, but they are attending post-secondary.
Not meaningful, not attending ps: this means they do have a MN employment record, but it isn’t meaningful and they are not attending post-secondary.
No MN emp record - not attending ps: this means they do not have a MN employment record nor are they attending a post-secondary institution.

The other designation is “After 2023” which will be filtered out whenever I analyze the dataset.
Let’s first take a look at the proportion of individuals that are in each state at each grad.year.x.
The chart below shows the proportion of individuals in the dataset that are in each “state/path” for each grad.year.x. There are a couple of things to highlight.

The percentage of individuals with meaningful employment in Northwest Minnesota peaks about 6 years after college with nearly 25% of graduates working 1,000 hours or more that year for a single employer in the Northwest Minnesota.
At 6 years after graduating, over 40% of the graduates have meaningful employment in Minnesota.
Graduates not reaching the meaningful employment threshold in Minnesota hovers around 15% after the first year of graduating high school.
Over 40% of graduates disappear from my dataset about 10 years after graduating. This means they are not going to post-secondary in the United States and do not have a Minnesota employment record.
